High-resolution XPS Ti 2p spectra (b) and UV–vis absorption spectra (c) of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs. (d) Photocatalytic degradation of RhB by CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs under visible light. (e) HRTEM image of CQDs/TiO2 NPs.

Effects of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs on growth inhibition (a), chlorophyll ɑ content (b), and esterase activity (c) in S. obliquus. (d) Immobilization rate of D. magna induced by CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs. (e) Semiquantitative scoring of swim bladder inflation defects in zebrafish larvae. (f) Representative images of swim bladder inflation. * represents p < 0.05 between the NPs exposed group and the control. # represents p < 0.05 between the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs at the same concentration.

Effects of CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs on the ROS level (a, b, c), SOD activity (d, e, f) and CAT level (g, h, i) in S. obliquus, D. magna and D. rerio. To avoid interference from the CQDs, we replaced the enzymatic activity of CAT with the mRNA expression in S. obliquus. * represents p < 0.05 between the NPs exposed group and the control. # represents p < 0.05 between the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs at the same concentration.

(a) Decomposition rate of H2O2 under catalysis by the CQDs, TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs. Different letters beside the data represent significant differences (p < 0.05). (b) Time-dependent ·OH generation by CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs under visible light irradiation. ·OH radicals were quantified by the fluorescence of terephthalic acid (TA) byproduct. + .

Accumulation and depuration of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs in organisms. The data represent the means ± standard deviations (SD). Different letters beside the data represent significant differences within each species or organ.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modification reduces TiO2 nanoparticle toxicity in an aquatic food chain

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Environment Research Institute, Shandong University, Qingdao 266237, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Environment Research Institute, Shandong University, Qingdao, 266237, China

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic removal of pollutants has emerged as a promising technology for environmental remediation due to its high efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and the absence of additional chemicals requrirements [1]. Among various photocatalysts, titanium dioxide nanoparticles (TiO2 NPs) are the most widely used owing to their excellent performance, stable chemical properties, and low cost [2]. However, the large bandgap of TiO2 NPs (anatase 3.2 eV, rutile 3.0 eV) and the high recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes result in a low catalytic capacity under visible light [2]. To overcome these limitations, researchers have integrated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with TiO2 NPs via hydrothermal or impregnation methods [3]. CQDs have unique upconverted photoluminescence properties, enabling them to convert low-energy light (500 ≤λ≤1000 nm) into high-energy light (325 ≤λ≤425 nm), thus acting as photosensitizers in CQDs/TiO2 NPs to broaden the available spectral range [4,5]. Additionally, CQDs promote the rapid separation of photogenerated electrons from holes, inhibiting their recombination and significantly improving the energy utilization efficiency of TiO2 NPs [6,7]. Despite the widespread use of CQDs/TiO2 NPs in photocatalytic degradation [8,9], hydrogen evolution [10], and other applications [11,12], their potential toxicity remains poorly understood. The interaction between different materials in composites can alter their physicochemical properties and biological activities in unpredictable ways. Therefore, the toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 composites cannot be assessed simply from the results of the original nanomaterial [13].

When irradiated with appropriate wavelengths, TiO2 NPs generate abundant reactive oxygen species (ROS), which are effective in pollutant degradation but can also induce severe oxidative stress in living organisms [2,14]. While the incorporation of CQDs into TiO2 NPs extends the available spectral range and enhances light utilization, which promotes greater ROS production [3], this could potentially increase toxicity. However, CQDs can utilize abundant surface functional groups as catalytic sites to scavenge free radicals efficiently [15]. This enzyme-like activity of CQDs, akin to that of antioxidant enzymes in organisms, has been shown to help mitigate oxidative stress [16,17]. Consequently, while CQDs doping might increase ROS production, it also has the potential to mitigate it. Furthermore, the loading of carbon-based nanomaterials could change the surface properties of nanocomposites [18], altering their interactions with organisms and toxicity [19]. These combined factors make the biological effects of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs complex and challenging to predict.

CQDs/TiO2 NPs are often used in water treatment and inevitably discharged into water bodies [20], making it crucial to understand their toxicity to aquatic organisms. The organisms chosen for this study—phytoplankton (Scenedesmus obliquus), zooplankton (Daphnia magna), and fish (Danio rerio)—represent producers, primary consumers, and secondary consumers, respectively. These organisms are globally distributed and commonly used as model species in risk assessments [21,22]. More importantly, they form a food chain, enabling the exploration of bioaccumulation potential and biomagnification of NPs, which are critical but often overlooked issues in ecotoxicology.

This study aims to investigate the effects of CQDs loading on the toxicity of TiO2 NPs and the underlying mechanisms. We compared the toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs across various trophic levels, evaluated oxidative stress markers, and employed NPs enzyme activity assays, radical scavenging tests, and ROS fluorescent probes to explore the impact of CQDs loading on free radical production and scavenging. Further investigations into bioaccumulation, food chain transfer and Derjaguin–Landau–Verwey–Overbeek (DLVO) calculations demonstrated the influence of increased surface electronegativity on mitigating toxicity. This study advances the development of safer, more effective nano-catalysts by aligning technological innovation with ecological safety.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Synthesis and characterization of CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs NPs were synthesized via a bottom-up hydrothermal method using citric acid and thiourea as precursors [23]. CQDs/TiO2 NPs were synthesized hydrothermally by mixing 0.5 g P25-TiO2 NPs and 15 mg CQDs in 20 mL ultrapure water, stirring at room temperature for 4 hours, and then heating at 150°C for 6 hours [24]. Details of the synthesis methods are provided in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental animal cultivation and toxicity assessment of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: S. obliquus was maintained in sterilized BG11 medium under a 12:12 h light/dark cycle at 3000 lux and 25 ± 1°C in an illumination incubator. The initial cell density for exposure was 5 × 104 cells/mL using exponentially growing cells. NPs concentrations were 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, and 80 mg/L, and the solutions were sonicated for 10 minutes before exposure. Growth inhibition, chlorophyll a content, cell membrane integrity and esterase activity were tested using flow cytometry (FCM, Accuri C6 Plus, BD Biosciences, Singapore) at 1, 3, 5, and 7 days.

D. magna was reared in M7 medium with twice weekly medium changes. They were fed 1 × 105 cells mL−1 S. obliquus daily and maintained in an illumination incubator at 20 ± 1°C with a 16:8 h light/dark cycle at 2000 lux. Acute immobilization rates in D. magna were tested at NPs concentrations of 0, 1, 5, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mg/L, with a 72-hour exposure period [25]. Chronic toxicity of D. magna continued for 21 days to assess the adverse effects of low-dose (0, 1, 5, and 10 mg/L) and long-term exposure on the reproduction of D. magna. The endpoints of chronic toxicity included the number of days to first brood, the number of neonates in the first brood, the total number of neonates and the average number of offspring in each brood. For both acute and chronic exposure experiments, the NPs suspension was changed every 24 hours and sonicated for 10 minutes before use.

D. rerio (zebrafish, wild-type AB) was raised in a circulatory system at 28 ± 0.5°C with a 14 h:10 h light/dark cycle. The fish were fed newly hatched brine shrimp daily. Healthy females and males (1:2) were placed in a mating tank to spawn. Fertilized embryos were collected at 4 hours post-fertilization (hpf) and exposed to NPs at concentrations of 0, 10, 20, 50, and 100 mg/L. Each group of 50 embryos was placed in glass culture dishes containing 50 mL exposure solution. The NPs suspension was changed every 24 hours and sonicated for 10 minutes before use. The embryos were maintained in an illuminated incubator at temperature of 28 ± 0.5 °C, with light intensity of 2000 lux and 14 h:10 h light/dark cycle during exposure. Toxicity endpoints included mortality, hatch rate, heart rate, apoptosis, and swim bladder inflation defects.
Specific experimental methods can be found in the supporting information. All animal experiments were approved by the Ethics Committee of Shandong university (ECSBMSSDU2022–2–137).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of ROS levels and antioxidant responses in organisms
ce:section.ce:para: The levels of total ROS, ·OH and H2O2 in S. obliquus, D. magna, and D. rerio after exposure were quantified using H2DCFDA, HKOH-1r and HKPerox-2 fluorescence probes, respectively [26]. For S. obliquus, the fluorescence intensity was measured using FCM. For D. magna and D. rerio, fluorescence images were obtained using an inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti-U, Nikon, Japan), and then the fluorescence intensity was quantified with ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health, USA).

The SOD activities of S. obliquus, D. magna, and D. rerio were determined via the WST-1 method, and the CAT activities of D. magna and D. rerio were determined via the ammonium molybdate method, using commercial kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China). Owing to difficulties in separating CQDs/TiO2 NPs from algal cells and the inherent CAT-like activity of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs, the CAT activity in S. obliquus was substituted with the cat gene expression analysis. Detailed descriptions of the experimental approach are provided in the supporting information.

Rescue experiment was conducted to confirm the role of ROS in toxicity. N-acetylcysteine (NAC) was chosen as the ROS scavenger [27]. D. magna were exposed to: (1) 50 mg/L TiO2 NPs, (2) 50 mg/L CQDs/TiO2 NPs, or (3) 50 mg/L TiO2 NPs with 3 mg/L NAC. After 72 hours, the ROS levels and immobilization rate of D. magna were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessing the ability of NPs to scavenge and produce free radicals
Catalase-like activity of NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The catalase-like activity of NPs was measured by their ability to catalyze the H2O2 decomposition via a commercial kit (Nanjing Jiancheng Bioengineering Institute, China). NPs (1 mg/mL) were added to incubate with H2O2 for 10 min at 37°C, ammonium molybdate was added to stop the reaction, and reacted with the remaining H2O2 to produce a yellow complex with 405 nm absorption [16]. Finally, the catalase-like activity of the NPs was evaluated by calculating the degradation rate of H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of hydroxyl radical and photogenerated holes
ce:section.ce:para:
We selected TEMPO and DMPO as spin traps for h
+ and ⋅OH, respectively, and employed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, EMXnano, Bruker, Germany) to measure the generation of radicals by TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs under visible light. Specifically, TEMPO (0.1 mM) or DMPO (100 mM) was mixed with 300 mg/L NPs and was incubated at 25°C under 10,000 lux illumination in a light-incubated chamber for 30 minutes. The samples were then immediately analyzed using EPR.

Terephthalic acid (TA) can react with ·OH as a trapper and generate fluorescent byproducts [28], so the amount of ·OH was assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity (excitation-310 nm/emission-430 nm). The reaction was conducted by combining 6.19 mM TA with 100 mg/L NPs at 25°C under 10000 lux illumination. Every hour, 5 mL of the sample was taken and centrifuged to obtain the supernatant, which was then measured for fluorescence intensity using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical scavenger test
ce:section.ce:para:
The catalytic performance of CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs under visible light was assessed via the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB). To further investigate the conversion of photogenerated holes (h
+ ) and ·OH in NPs, we selected ethylenediamine tetra acetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and tert-butanol (TBA) as scavengers for h
+ and ·OH, respectively. A mixture of 1 mM scavengers, 100 mg/L NPs, and 20 ppm RhB was stirred for 30 minutes in the dark. The degradation rate was then assessed after 4 hours under 10,000 lux light and compared to a scavenger-free control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioconcentration of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: For S. obliquus, 1 × 106 cells mL−1 algae cells in the exponential growth period were inoculated and then incubated for 48 hours. TiO2 NPs or CQDs/TiO2 NPs were then added to yield a 10 mg/L exposure concentration. After 24 hours of exposure, algae cells and extracellular NPs were separated via a sucrose gradient (sucrose: water =1.6, 1.4, 1.2, 1.0, 0.8 and 0.6) and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min [29]. Then, the algal cells were collected and washed with PBS.

Before the accumulation experiment of D. magna, the daphnids (7–14 days) were transferred to clean M7 medium for 24 hours to empty their gut. One hundred daphnids were then exposed to 10 mg/L NPs in 1000 mL M7 medium. D. magna was collected after 24-hour exposure and carefully rinsed [30].

For the accumulation of D. rerio, adult zebrafish (4–5 months old) were purchased from Nanjing Yishulihua Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Zebrafish (female: male = 1: 1) were exposed to 10 mg/L NPs in 2 L medium for 7 days. D. rerio were fed daily, and the exposure medium was changed after feeding. After 7-day enrichment experiment, the fish were euthanized and dissected (gill, intestine, liver, ovary, brain, and muscle). The tissues of the six fish were mixed into one sample before subsequent processing. In addition, we performed a purification experiment on zebrafish. After the accumulation phase, these zebrafish (female: male = 1:1) were transferred to clean water for two-day purification.

The collected biological samples were dried, weighed, digested with nitric acid and further decomposed with a sulfuric acid–ammonium sulfate solution [30]. The titanium (IV) concentration was analyzed via an inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometer (ICP-MS, 7800, Agilent, USA). The recovery rate of this method was 83 % - 91 %, meeting the requirements for subsequent analyses. The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) were 0.99 mg/kg and 3.33 mg/kg, respectively. The details in the calculation of recovery rate, LOD and LOQ are provided in Text S5.

To exclude the influence of NPs sedimentation, the time-weighted mean concentration (CTWM) was used instead of the nominal concentration [31], with continuous monitoring of NPs agglomeration and deposition. The bioconcentration factor (BCF) was calculated by dividing the NPs concentration in the organism by the CTWM. The DLVO theory was used to analyze the colloidal interactions between algae cells and NPs [32]. Detailed descriptions of the CTWM and DLVO calculation are provided in the Text S6 and Text S7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trophic transfer from S. obliquus to D. rerio
ce:section.ce:para: Two trophic transfer assays (S. obliquus → D. magna and S. obliquus → D. magna → D. rerio) were conducted. First, S. obliquus accumulated TiO2 NPs or CQDs/TiO2 NPs via the method described in Section 2.5. After NPs exposure, the separated alga cells were immediately fed to D. magna (7–14 d) at a relatively high concentration (1 ×106 cells mL−1) to ensure adequate transmission. D. magna was fed daily for 2 days, then the D. magna was collected and rinsed for further experiments. One portion of D. magna was digested to determine the titanium (IV) concentration, whereas the other portion served as feed for zebrafish. Each zebrafish (> 6 months) was fed ten pre-treated D. magna daily for 7 days. One female and one male were picked to weight together and mixed into one sample for digestion and Ti measurement. The biomagnification factor (BMF) was calculated as the ratio of the Ti concentration in the predator to that in the prey [33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation (SD), unless otherwise specified. Statistical analyses were performed via IBM SPSS 22.0 software. Statistical significance (p < 0.05) was analyzed through one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: According to the TEM imaging, the average single particle size of CQDs/TiO2 NPs (38.59 ± 7.37 nm) is similar to that of TiO2 NPs (37.86 ± 10.28 nm) (Figure S1). In the HRTEM image, lattice spacings of approximately 0.35 nm and 0.24 nm were observed ( Fig. 1e), corresponding to the (101) plane of TiO2 and the (100) plane of graphite, respectively [34]. Additionally, SEM and EDX mapping revealed the presence of C, N, and S on the TiO2 NPs surface (Figure S2), further confirming the successful formation of the CQDs/TiO2 composite. The characteristic FTIR bands of CQDs/TiO2 NPs at 1401 cm−1 (CO) and 1709 cm−1 (C-N) confirmed the presence of CQDs (Fig. 1a). Additionally, the shift of the Ti 2p peak to higher binding energies in the XPS spectra (Figure S3) provided further evidence of CQDs incorporation [35]. Thermogravimetric analysis revealed a 1.71 % mass loss for TiO2 NPs and a 3.71 % mass loss for the CQDs/TiO2 NPs, with an additional 2.00 % attributed to the combustion of the CQDs (Fig. 1b). The enhanced visible light absorption (Fig. 1c) and accelerated RhB degradation (Fig. 1d) further confirmed the successful synthesis of CQDs/TiO2 NPs. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra and transient photocurrent (PC) measurements showed the improvement of photoelectrochemical properties of CQDs/TiO2 NPs (Figure S4a, b). CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibit lower PL intensity compared to TiO2 NPs (Figure S4a), indicating the enhanced electron-hole separation. The higher transient photocurrent of CQDs/TiO2 NPs demonstrates that CQDs doping suppresses electron-hole recombination (Figure S4b).

The band gap of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs was determined using Tauc analysis based on the UV-Vis DRS data [36]. The results show that their band gap (3.08 eV) is slightly lower than that of TiO2 NPs (3.17 eV) (Figure S5), enabling CQDs/TiO2 NPs to utilize visible light more effectively for catalytic reactions [37]. Notably, CQDs doping significantly enhances the electronegativity of TiO2 NPs, reducing their hydrodynamic diameter (Table S2). However, the CQDs decoration and hydrothermal treatment did not alter the morphology (Figure S1), surface area (Figure S4c), pore structure (Figure S4d), or crystallinity of TiO2 NPs (Figure S4e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparisons of CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs toxicity to S. obliquus, D. magna and D. rerio
ce:section.ce:para: The toxicity of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs to freshwater organisms was evaluated in three species (S. obliquus, D. magna, and D. rerio) representing different trophic levels. For S. obliquus (freshwater algae), growth inhibition, chlorophyll a content, esterase activity and cell membrane integrity were detected. The growth curves (Figure S6a) and corresponding growth inhibition rates ( Fig. 2a) indicate that the CQDs/TiO2 NPs were significantly less inhibitory to algal growth compared to unmodified TiO2 NPs. Specifically, 80 mg/L TiO2 NPs caused nearly 90 % growth inhibition on the first day, whereas 80 mg/L CQDs/TiO2 NPs resulted in only 50 % growth inhibition, a 40 % reduction. Interestingly, on the first day of exposure, 1 mg/L and 5 mg/L TiO2 NPs showed negative growth inhibition rates, suggesting the faster algal growth after TiO2 NPs exposure. This phenomenon is often attributed to a low-dose stimulation effect [13,38]. Since photosynthesis is essential for algal proliferation, it was evaluated by measuring the chlorophyll a content [39]. Both CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs reduced the chlorophyll a levels on days 5 and 7, but the TiO2 NPs caused a more severe reduction (Fig. 2b). Esterase activity, an indicator of metabolic activity, showed that both CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs had a hormesis effect, but the CQDs doping mitigated the stimulus caused by the TiO2 NPs (Fig. 2c). The integrity of algae cell membranes was tested because they are especially vulnerable to xenobiotics [40]. However, neither CQDs/TiO2 NPs nor TiO2 NPs compromised the integrity of the cell membrane during the exposure period (Figure S6b).

For D. magna (zooplankton), both acute immobilization test and reproduction test were conducted. The 72-hour acute immobilization test revealed the EC50 values of 31.62 mg/L for TiO2 NPs and 95.50 mg/L for CQDs/TiO2 NPs (Fig. 2d), which clearly demonstrated the less toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs. In the 21-day reproduction test (Table S3), the average number of offspring per brood decreased from 25.22 (control group) to 17.12 (10 mg/L TiO2 NPs) and 20.93 (10 mg/L CQDs/TiO2 NPs). The total number of neonates decreased from 130.8 (control group) to 91.80 (10 mg/L TiO2 NPs) and 115.60 (10 mg/L CQDs/TiO2 NPs), confirming that the effect of pristine TiO2 NPs was significantly greater than that of CQDs/TiO2 NPs.

For D. rerio (fish), mortality, hatch rate, heart rate, apoptosis, and swim bladder inflation defects were examined. In general, the negative effect of the two nanoparticles on D. rerio was weaker than that on S. obliquus and D. magna. Even at 100 mg/L, neither the CQDs/TiO2 NPs nor the TiO2 NPs induced significant hatching delays, heart rate alterations, or embryonic death in the zebrafish embryos (Figure S7a, b, c). However, both the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs caused increased apoptosis (Figure S7d) and bladder inflation defects (Fig. 2e). Notably, the CQDs/TiO2 NPs caused significantly fewer swim bladder defects than did the TiO2 NPs. Specifically, 78 % of the larvae exhibited swim bladder defects when exposed to 100 mg/L TiO2 NPs, whereas only 38 % of the larvae exhibited defects when exposed to the CQDs/TiO2 NPs.

In this study, D. magna was the most sensitive organism, with as low as 1 mg/L of TiO2 NPs affecting its reproductive capabilities, consistent with other research exploring the toxicity of NPs or organic pollutants [22,41]. This heightened sensitivity can be attributed to D. magna's unique physiological structure (its chitin polysaccharide exoskeleton readily adsorbs organic pollutants) [41] and feeding habits (which facilitate the accumulation of NPs during filter feeding) [42]. Contrasting this, Yao et al. found S. obliquus was more sensitive to CQDs than D. magna [43], demonstrating that responses vary among different pollutants and highlighting the necessity of using multiple trophic levels in toxicity studies. In summary, the toxicity assessments across the aquatic food chain of S. obliquus, D. magna, and D. rerio indicate that while the NPs exhibit varying degrees of impact, CQDs doping significantly alleviated TiO2 NPs toxicity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CAT-like activity of CQDs alleviates TiO2 NPs induced oxidative stress
ce:section.ce:para: Under illumination, TiO2 NPs generate a large amount of ROS [44], which attack biomolecules and disrupt normal metabolic processes [45]. We evaluated the oxidative stress caused by the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and TiO2 NPs by measuring the ROS level and antioxidant enzyme activity (SOD and CAT) in S. obliquus, D. magna, and D. rerio. Both types of NPs caused a dose-dependent increase in the ROS levels across all three organisms ( Fig. 3a-c). Notably, TiO2 NPs induced a significantly higher ROS elevation than CQDs/TiO2 NPs at equivalent concentrations. For antioxidant enzymes, the increase in SOD activity and CAT gene expression in S. obliquus induced by TiO2 exposure was significantly alleviated by the addition of the CQDs, and similar trends were observed in D. magna and D. rerio (Fig. 3d-i). Interestingly, the CAT activity in zebrafish exposed to CQDs/TiO2 NPs decreased rather than increased (Fig. 3i). A similar reduction was observed with CQDs exposure alone [46], likely because the CQDs competitively metabolize H2O2 and inhibit CAT transcription and expression. This implies that the inherent CAT-like activity of CQDs plays a crucial role in mitigating oxidative stress.

The main physiological function of CAT is to catalyze the degradation of H2O2 [47]. Therefore, we evaluated the CAT-like activity of the CQDs, TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs in decomposing H2O2 ( Fig. 4a). Both CQDs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibited strong activity in H2O2 decomposition, in contrast to the weak catalytic ability of the TiO2 NPs. The degradation rate of H2O2 by CQDs/TiO2 NPs increased dramatically from 1.3 × 10−3 μmol/min (TiO2 NPs) to 24.97 × 10−3 μmol/min. Using H2O2 fluorescent probes, we quantified H2O2 level in organisms. Compared to TiO2 NPs, the H2O2 levels of all three species exposed to CQDs/TiO2 NPs were significantly lower ( Fig. 5a-c), further confirming the CAT-like activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs. While the CAT-like activity of CQDs has been reported [16,17], our study demonstrates the antioxidant capabilities of CQDs complexes for the first time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CQDs doping suppresses the generation of ·OH by TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxyl radical (·OH) is the primary product of TiO2 NPs under visible light irradiation and a major contributor to oxidative stress [45,48]. In this study, we used EPR to monitor the generation of ⋅OH by TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs under visible light. As shown in Fig. 4c, after 30-minute illumination, TiO2 NPs generate stronger DMPO-⋅OH signals than CQDs/TiO2 NPs. Moreover, CQDs/TiO2 and TiO2 NPs can continuously produce ·OH, as measured by terephthalic acid (TA) (Fig. 4b). The reaction conforms to the time-proportional relationship following zero-order reaction kinetics. Therefore, the generation rate of ·OH can be assessed by comparing the slopes of the fitted lines [28]. Surprisingly, despite exhibiting stronger catalytic ability, the CQDs/TiO2 NPs resulted in a lower ·OH production rate (217.39) than TiO2 NPs (547.89). We also quantified the ·OH radical levels in the organisms and observed a similar trend. The ·OH concentration was significantly lower after exposure to CQDs/TiO2 NPs compared to TiO2 NPs, particularly in S. obliquus and D. rerio (Fig. 5d-f).

The ·OH generation in the TiO2 NPs suspension primarily arises from the conversion of h
+
[44]. Therefore, the decrease in the ·OH level may be due to the inhibition of this conversion process. Using TEMPO as a spin trap for h
+ , we observed that the decrease in TEMPO signal caused by CQDs/TiO2 NPs was greater than that of TiO2 NPs (Fig. 4d), indicating that CQDs/TiO2 NPs generated more h
+ under visible light. In addition, using RhB as an indicator, we introduced a ·OH scavenger (TBA) and an h
+ scavenger (EDTA-2Na) during the catalytic degradation process (Figure S9). After the addition of the ·OH scavenger, the RhB degradation rate with TiO2 NPs decreased to 72.4 %, whereas it only decreased to 86 % with CQDs/TiO2 NPs. This suggests that ·OH plays a less significant role in the catalytic activity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs compared with TiO2 NPs. However, the addition of the h
+ scavenger reduced the degradation rate to 80.4 % with TiO2 NPs and to 64.2 % with the CQDs/TiO2 NPs (Figure S9), indicating a more significant role of h
+ in CQDs/TiO2 NPs catalyzed degradation. This shift implies that the CQDs/TiO2 NPs are more likely to directly utilize h
+ in the catalytic reactions rather than converting it into ·OH. Unlike ·OH dispersed in solution, h
+ exists only on the surface of the NPs [49], and electrostatic repulsion between the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and the same negatively charged bio-interface prevents further interaction of h
+ with the organism [19]. Thus, CQDs/TiO2 can more efficiently utilize absorbed energy for pollutant degradation rather than causing oxidative stress in organisms.

To confirm that the reduced toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs results from alleviating oxidative stress, we conducted a rescue experiment using D. magna. As shown in Figure S8, NAC recovered the TiO2 NPs induced ROS elevation, also mitigated the TiO2 NPs induced acute immobilization (from 52.5 % to 15.0 % immobilization). These findings confirm that oxidative stress is a key factor in TiO2 NPs-induced toxicity. CQDs doping not only catalyzed H2O2 degradation but also inhibited ·OH generation (Fig. 4). Hence, CQDs-doped NPs alleviate their toxicity by reducing oxidative stress.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioaccumulation of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the bioaccumulation of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs by S. obliquus, D. magna and D. rerio. Both types of NPs were found to accumulate in S. obliquus ( Table 1). The accumulated titanium (Ti) content (101.73 mg/kg) due to TiO2 NPs exposure is 2.3 times greater than that for CQDs/TiO2 NPs(45.07 mg/kg). In D. rerio, NPs accumulation in organs followed the order of intestine > liver > gill > brain > ovary ≈ muscle (Table 1). Notably, the Ti content in the intestine after TiO2 NPs exposure (12526.47 mg/kg) was significantly greater than that after CQDs/TiO2 NPs exposure (6543.55 mg/kg). After purification (recovery in clean water for 2 days), most of the NPs were excreted, with residues primarily in the liver and intestine. Crucially, TiO2 NPs are more difficult to excrete by D. rerio than CQDs/TiO2 NPs, as evidenced by the greater proportion of the remaining Ti in D. rerio exposing to TiO2 NPs (Table 1). The reduced bioconcentration of the CQDs/TiO2 NPs in S. obliquus and D. rerio is likely due to the increased electronegativity of the TiO2 NPs surface from CQDs doping (Table S2).

This hypothesis is supported by the results of DLVO modeling for the interaction energies between algal cells and NPs. The energy barrier consists mainly of electrostatic repulsion (Figure S10). The CQDs/TiO2 NPs, with a more electronegative surface, exhibited a significantly greater maximum energy barrier (TiO2 NPs:132.21 kT, CQDs/TiO2 NPs: 292.35 kT). This increased repulsion between the CQDs/TiO2 NPs and negatively charged biological surfaces, reducing NPs accumulation and facilitating their excretion [19,40]. This lower internal exposure concentration is another important reason for the reduced toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs.

For D. magna, the Ti concentration did not differ significantly between exposures to TiO2 NPs (44,153.01 mg/kg) and CQDs/TiO2 NPs (42,731.61 mg/kg), although the Ti concentration in D. magna was orders of magnitude greater than that in S. obliquus and D. rerio (Table 1). High NP uptake adversely affects the nutritional absorption and development of D. magna, making it the most sensitive species to TiO2 NPs exposure [50]. The high NPs concentration was likely due to D. magna’s filter-feeding behavior. D. magna filters a large amount of water through combs of setae in the carapace and separates 0.4–40 μm particles into the intestine as food [51]. The amount of filtered NPs is mainly determined by the size of the NPs rather than the surface properties [52]. However, CQDs doping did not alter the NPs agglomeration or the concentration changes of NPs during exposure (Figure S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Trophic transfer of TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Two trophic transfer assays of NPs (S. obliquus → D. magna and S. obliquus → D. magna → D. rerio) were conducted. The algae were preincubated with NPs as the initial food source. After being fed with algae for two days, the Ti concentration in D. magna was 10.85 mg/kg for TiO2 NPs and 10.98 mg/kg for the CQDs/TiO2 NPs (Table S4). The BMF during trophic transfer was far smaller than 1 (0.106 for TiO2 NPs and 0.243 for CQDs/TiO2 NPs), indicating nearly no biomagnification occurred during trophic transfer. When the NPs were further transferred from D. magna to D. rerio, no TiO2 NPs or CQDs/TiO2 NPs were detected in D. rerio (Table S4). These findings suggest that both TiO2 NPs and CQDs/TiO2 NPs exhibit limited transmission through the food chain, likely because these NPs are insoluble in water and lipids and rarely release ions, preventing long-term retention in organisms [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This study focuses on a critical but previously overlooked aspect of CQDs/TiO2 NPs: their biological effects and underlying mechanisms. Notably, CQDs doping not only enhanced the photocatalytic ability of TiO2 NPs but also reduced their toxicity to aquatic organisms (S. obliquus, D. magna and D. rerio). The reduction in toxicity can be attributed to the CAT-like activity of the CQDs, which plays a critical role in mitigating oxidative stress. Additionally, CQDs doping also reduced the generation of ·OH radicals from CQDs/TiO2 NPs under light irradiation, mitigating the oxidative stress induced by TiO2 NPs. The changes in the surface potential of the nanocomposite caused by CQDs doping reduced the bioaccumulation of the NPs, which is another crucial reason for the reduced toxicity of CQDs/TiO2 NPs. We are the first to systematically investigate the aquatic toxicity, bioaccumulation and food chain transfer of CQDs/TiO2 NPs, and both the observed phenomena and their underlying mechanisms are reported for the first time. These data bridge a critical knowledge gap concerning CQDs/TiO2 NPs toxicity and provide an important proof-of-principle for the development of CQDs-related nanocomposites. Notably, the results of this study may not fully represent the risks associated with low-concentration and long-term exposure. Therefore, future studies should utilize more sensitive approaches, such as omics technologies, to provide more comprehensive ecological risk assessments in real-world ecosystems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Environmental Implication
ce:section.ce:para: To improve catalytic efficiency and degrade persistent pollutants, composite nano-catalysts are increasingly developed, but corresponding toxicological studies remain insufficient. This study explored the effects of CQDs loading on TiO2 NPs' biological impacts. Results showed that CQDs loading not only enhanced catalytic efficiency but also significantly reduced toxicity. Mechanistic studies revealed that CQDs alleviate oxidative stress, reduce free radical release, and lower bioconcentration. This research provides key insights into assessing the ecological risks of CQDs composites and developing safer, more efficient nano-catalysts.
